era_context: The early 18th century, during the reign of Peter the Great in Russia (final years) and the Yongzheng Emperor in Qing China (early years). Europe was in a period of relative peace following the War of Spanish Succession (1701–1713), though with significant diplomatic tensions. The Ottoman Empire remained a major power. European colonial expansion was accelerating, with Britain, France, Spain, and the Dutch competing for dominance in the Americas, Africa, and Asia. This was a period of intellectual ferment in Europe, with the Enlightenment beginning to take shape.

events.title: Death of the Duke of Orléans; end of the French Regency
events.description: On 2 December 1723, Philippe II, Duke of Orléans, died at the age of 49. The Duke of Orléans had served as regent of France during the minority of Louis XV (1715–1723), a period known as the Regency. The Duke's death marked the formal end of the Regency period, though Louis XV, who was only 13 years old at the time, did not immediately assume full personal rule. The Duke of Orléans had been a controversial figure, known for his libertine lifestyle and his conflicts with the French nobility and the church. His death was followed by a period of instability in French politics, as various factions competed for influence over the young king. The Duke's death is often seen as marking the end of an era in French history.
key_figures: Philippe II, Duke of Orléans (regent, deceased)

sources.name: French court records and contemporary accounts

events.certainty_note: The Duke of Orléans's death date is well-documented in French court records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Yongzheng Emperor consolidates power; purges court rivals
events.description: In 1723, the Yongzheng Emperor (r. 1722–1735) continued his aggressive consolidation of power in Qing China. He purged court officials and members of the imperial household whom he suspected of disloyalty or ambition. The Yongzheng Emperor also expanded the Grand Council (Junjichu), a body of trusted advisors that reported directly to the emperor, thereby reducing the power of the traditional imperial bureaucracy. These actions consolidated the Yongzheng Emperor's power but also created a climate of fear and resentment at court. The purges were justified by the emperor as necessary for the stability of the realm, but they were controversial and generated criticism from Confucian scholars. The Yongzheng Emperor's consolidation of power in 1723 set the stage for his broader program of administrative reform.

sources.name: Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty (Qing Shilu)

events.certainty_note: The Yongzheng Emperor's consolidation of power is well-documented in Qing state records and later historiography.

events.title: Treaty of Constantinople signed between Russia and the Ottoman Empire
events.description: In 1723, Russia and the Ottoman Empire signed the Treaty of Constantinople, which attempted to regulate their relations and resolve disputes over territorial boundaries and commercial rights. The treaty was negotiated in the context of Peter the Great's recent conquest of the Caucasian coast of the Caspian Sea (1722–1723) and the Ottoman Empire's concerns about Russian expansion. The treaty recognized Russian control over the conquered territories but also included provisions for Ottoman influence in the region. However, the treaty was short-lived and did not prevent subsequent conflicts between Russia and the Ottoman Empire. The treaty is significant as an attempt by both powers to establish a stable relationship, but it ultimately failed to resolve their fundamental conflicts of interest.
events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Constantinople is well-documented in diplomatic archives and contemporary records.

events.title: Establishment of the Perpetual Diet in Poland-Lithuania
events.description: In 1723, the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth established the Perpetual Diet (Sejm Nadzwyczajny), a permanent legislative body designed to address the chronic instability and dysfunction of the traditional Polish diet system. The Perpetual Diet was intended to provide more stable governance and to reduce the power of individual magnates who had previously been able to block legislation through the liberum veto (the right of any single member to veto any decision). However, the Perpetual Diet ultimately failed to achieve its goals, and the liberum veto remained in effect. The establishment of the Perpetual Diet is significant as an attempt to reform the Polish-Lithuanian political system, but it ultimately did not prevent the gradual decline of the Commonwealth.
key_figures: Augustus II (King of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania)

sources.name: Polish-Lithuanian state records and diet proceedings

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Perpetual Diet in 1723 is documented in Polish-Lithuanian records, though the exact date and details are somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Dummer's War begins in New England; conflict between English colonists and Native Americans
events.description: In 1723, Dummer's War (also called the Fourth Indian War or Lovewell's War) began in New England, a conflict between English colonists and Native American tribes, particularly the Abenaki and other Algonquian-speaking peoples. The war was triggered by disputes over land, trade, and colonial expansion. The conflict was characterized by raids and counter-raids, with both sides suffering casualties. The war lasted until 1726 and resulted in the displacement of many Native Americans from their traditional lands. Dummer's War is significant as one of the last major conflicts between English colonists and Native Americans in New England before the larger conflicts of the mid-18th century. The war demonstrated the increasing power of English colonists and the declining ability of Native Americans to resist colonial expansion.
key_figures: Abenaki leaders (names not well-documented in English sources)
events.certainty_note: Dummer's War is well-documented in colonial records and contemporary accounts, though Native American perspectives are underrepresented.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Treaty of Constantinople (1723-003) has been debated by historians. Some scholars argue that it was less consequential than once believed, as both Russia and the Ottoman Empire continued to pursue independent foreign policies and the treaty did not prevent subsequent conflicts. The treaty is sometimes presented in older historiography as a major diplomatic achievement, but modern scholarship suggests it was more of a temporary accommodation that quickly unraveled. Additionally, the extent to which the Perpetual Diet (1723-004) represented a genuine reform of Polish-Lithuanian governance has been questioned; some historians argue that it was largely ineffective and did not address the fundamental structural problems of the Commonwealth.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1723 is rich, particularly for European and East Asian events. French court records and diplomatic correspondence are abundant, and Chinese imperial records are comprehensive. However, the record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history. The perspectives of common people, merchants, and non-elite populations are underrepresented. The record for North America is dominated by English colonial sources; Native American perspectives are almost entirely absent or filtered through colonial accounts. The Russian and Ottoman sources reflect the perspectives of the centralizing states and may downplay internal dissent or administrative failures.

graph_edges.note: The death of the Duke of Orléans in 1723 led to Louis XV's assumption of personal rule in 1724
Yongzheng's consolidation of power in 1723 was part of his broader program of purges and administrative reform
The Treaty of Constantinople (1723) was undermined by subsequent diplomatic realignments that led to the Russo-Turkish War
The Perpetual Diet (1723) was an attempt to reform the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, which ultimately declined and was partitioned in 1795
Dummer's War (1723–1726) was a conflict between English colonists and Native Americans in New England
